***Law & Order*** is an American [police procedural](/wiki/Police_procedural "Police procedural") and [legal drama](/wiki/Legal_drama "Legal drama") television series created by [Dick Wolf](/wiki/Dick_Wolf "Dick Wolf") and produced by [Wolf Entertainment](/wiki/Wolf_Entertainment "Wolf Entertainment") and [Universal Television](/wiki/Universal_Television "Universal Television"), launching the [*Law & Order* franchise](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_(franchise) "Law & Order (franchise)").[[1]](#cite_note-2)

*Law & Order* aired its entire run on NBC, premiering on September 13, 1990, and completing its 20th season on May 24, 2010.[[2]](#cite_note-canceled-3)[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes_Cancel-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5) On September 28, 2021, after an 11-year hiatus, NBC announced that the series would be revived for a 21st season, which premiered on February 24, 2022. The revival saw the debut of new regular cast members, and the reprise of two roles by series veterans: District Attorney [Jack McCoy](/wiki/Jack_McCoy "Jack McCoy") ([Sam Waterston](/wiki/Sam_Waterston "Sam Waterston")), and Detective [Kevin Bernard](/wiki/Kevin_Bernard "Kevin Bernard") ([Anthony Anderson](/wiki/Anthony_Anderson "Anthony Anderson")).[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-TVLineRevive-7)[[7]](#cite_note-Revival_Premiere_Date-8) Afterwards, the series was renewed for three additional seasons.[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-S23Premiere-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) In May 2025, it was announced the series had been renewed for its [twenty-fifth season](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_season_25 "Law & Order season 25"), which premiered on September 25, 2025.

Set and filmed in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), the series follows a two-part approach: the first half hour is the investigation of a crime (usually murder) and apprehension of a suspect by [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") homicide detectives, while the second half is the prosecution of the defendant by the [Manhattan district attorney's office](/wiki/Manhattan_district_attorney%27s_office "Manhattan district attorney's office"). Plots are based on real cases that recently made headlines, although the motivation for the crime and the perpetrator may be much different.

The show started using revolving door casting in season two. The progression of the record for longest serving main cast members of the series is: [Jerry Orbach](/wiki/Jerry_Orbach "Jerry Orbach") as Detective [Lennie Briscoe](/wiki/Lennie_Briscoe "Lennie Briscoe"), [S. Epatha Merkerson](/wiki/S._Epatha_Merkerson "S. Epatha Merkerson") as Lieutenant [Anita Van Buren](/wiki/Anita_Van_Buren "Anita Van Buren") and [Sam Waterston](/wiki/Sam_Waterston "Sam Waterston") as Executive Assistant District Attorney/District Attorney [Jack McCoy](/wiki/Jack_McCoy "Jack McCoy").

*Law & Order*'s twenty-five seasons are second only to its spin-off *[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")* (1999–present) for the [longest-running live action scripted American primetime series](/wiki/List_of_longest-running_scripted_American_primetime_television_series "List of longest-running scripted American primetime television series"). The success of the series has led to the creation of additional shows, making *Law & Order* a franchise, including a television film, several video games, and international adaptations of the series. It has won and has been nominated for numerous awards over the years, including a number of [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards").

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Production")]

### History and development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History and development")]

In 1988, [Dick Wolf](/wiki/Dick_Wolf "Dick Wolf") developed a concept for a new television series that was going to depict a relatively optimistic picture of the American criminal justice system. He initially toyed with the idea of calling it *Night & Day* but then hit upon the title *Law & Order*. The first half of each episode was going to follow two homicide detectives (a senior and a junior [detective](/wiki/Detective "Detective")) and their commanding officer as they were investigating a violent crime.

The second half of the episode was going to follow the district attorney's office and the courts as two prosecutors, with advice from the district attorney, were attempting to convict the accused. *Law & Order* was going to be able to investigate some of the larger issues of the day by focusing on stories that were based on real cases making headlines.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199917-14)

Wolf took the idea to then-president of [Universal Television](/wiki/Universal_Television "Universal Television") [Kerry McCluggage](/wiki/Kerry_McCluggage "Kerry McCluggage"), who pointed out the similarity to a 1963 series titled *[Arrest and Trial](/wiki/Arrest_and_Trial "Arrest and Trial")*, which lasted one season. The two watched the pilot of that series, in which a police officer ([Ben Gazzara](/wiki/Ben_Gazzara "Ben Gazzara")) arrested a man for armed robbery in the first half, and the defense attorney, played by [Chuck Connors](/wiki/Chuck_Connors "Chuck Connors"), gets the perpetrator off as the wrong guy in the second half; this was the formula of the show every week.

Wolf decided that he wanted a fresh approach to the genre. His detectives occasionally were going to be fallible. He wanted to go from police procedural to prosecution with a greater degree of realism. The prosecution was going to be the hero, a reversal of the usual formula in lawyer dramas.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199917–18-15)

[Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") initially ordered 13 episodes based on the concept alone, with no pilot. Then-network head [Barry Diller](/wiki/Barry_Diller "Barry Diller") reversed the decision. He did not believe it was a "Fox show", although he loved the idea. Wolf then went to [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), which ordered a pilot, "[Everybody's Favorite Bagman](/wiki/Everybody%27s_Favorite_Bagman "Everybody's Favorite Bagman")", written by Wolf about corrupt city officials involved with the mob. The network liked the pilot but did not order it because there were no breakout stars.

NBC's top executives, [Brandon Tartikoff](/wiki/Brandon_Tartikoff "Brandon Tartikoff") and [Warren Littlefield](/wiki/Warren_Littlefield "Warren Littlefield"), screened the pilot and liked it in the summer of 1989. They were concerned that the intensity of the series was not going to be able to be repeated week after week.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199917–18-15) NBC executives had enough confidence by 1990 that the innovative show may appeal to a wide audience, and they ended up ordering the series for a full season.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199921–22-16)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Filming")]

The series is shot on location in New York City and is known for its extensive use of [local color](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/local_color "wikt:local color").[[16]](#cite_note-APMercury-17) The interior sets were located at [Chelsea Piers](/wiki/Chelsea_Piers "Chelsea Piers"). For the revived version starting with Season 21, sets were reconstructed in Brooklyn.[[17]](#cite_note-18) In early episodes courtroom scenes were shot at [Tweed Courthouse](/wiki/Tweed_Courthouse "Tweed Courthouse") before a courtroom set was built.[[18]](#cite_note-19) The exterior of the fictional 27th precinct police station was shot outside the New York County Surrogate's on Chambers St, while hospital scenes were filmed at the VA New York Harbour Medical Centre on East 23rd St, and Goldwater Memorial Hospital before its demolition.

In later seasons, New York City mayors [Rudy Giuliani](/wiki/Rudy_Giuliani "Rudy Giuliani") and [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg"), attorney [William Kunstler](/wiki/William_Kunstler "William Kunstler") and Bronx Congressman [José Serrano](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Enrique_Serrano "José Enrique Serrano") all appeared on the show as themselves. Local personalities also had recurring cameos as fictional characters, such as [Donna Hanover](/wiki/Donna_Hanover "Donna Hanover") and [Fran Lebowitz](/wiki/Fran_Lebowitz "Fran Lebowitz") as judges. On September 14, 2004, in New York City, a road leading to Pier 62 at Chelsea Piers (where the series was mostly shot) was renamed "Law & Order Way" in tribute to the series.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-Street-21)

The music for *Law & Order* was composed by veteran composer [Mike Post](/wiki/Mike_Post "Mike Post") and was deliberately designed to be minimal to match the abbreviated style of the series.[[21]](#cite_note-Courrier_and_Green_69-22) Post wrote the theme song using electric piano, guitar, and clarinet. In addition, [scene](/wiki/Scene_(fiction) "Scene (fiction)") changes were accompanied by a tone generated by Post. He refers to the tone as "The Clang",[[22]](#cite_note-Entertainment_Weekly_1993-02-26-23) while *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* critic [Ken Tucker](/wiki/Ken_Tucker "Ken Tucker") has referred to the sound as the "ominous *chung CHUNG*",[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199969-24) actor [Dann Florek](/wiki/Dann_Florek "Dann Florek") (in a promo) as the "doink doink",[[24]](#cite_note-25) and [Richard Belzer](/wiki/Richard_Belzer "Richard Belzer") as "the Dick Wolf Cash Register Sound".[[25]](#cite_note-Belzer_On_the_Media-26)

According to writer David Allan, 2021:

"The tone moves the viewer from scene to scene, jumping forward in time with all the importance and immediacy of a judge's gavel – which is exactly what Post was aiming for when he created it. While reminiscent of a jail door slamming..."[[26]](#cite_note-27)

But according to authors Susan Green and Randee Dawn:

"...it is actually an [amalgamation](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/amalgamation "wikt:amalgamation") of 'six or seven' sounds, including the sound made by 500 Japanese men walking across a hardwood floor."[[22]](#cite_note-Entertainment_Weekly_1993-02-26-23) The sound has become so associated with the *Law & Order* brand that it was also carried over to other series of the franchise."[[27]](#cite_note-Green_and_Dawn_60-28)

The British-aired [Channel Five](/wiki/Channel_5_(British_TV_channel) "Channel 5 (British TV channel)") versions of seasons 7–16 of *Law & Order*[[28]](#cite_note-29) feature the song "I'm Not Driving Anymore" by [Rob Dougan](/wiki/Rob_Dougan "Rob Dougan") in the opening credits, while seasons 17–20 used the American theme.

### Pilot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pilot")]

For the 1988 pilot, [George Dzundza](/wiki/George_Dzundza "George Dzundza") and [Chris Noth](/wiki/Chris_Noth "Chris Noth") were cast as the original detectives, Sergeant [Max Greevey](/wiki/Max_Greevey "Max Greevey") and Detective [Mike Logan](/wiki/Mike_Logan_(Law_%26_Order) "Mike Logan (Law & Order)").[[29]](#cite_note-Courrier_and_Green_25-30) The producers felt that Dzundza would be a perfect senior police officer as he was someone the producers felt they could see themselves riding along with in a police cruiser.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999110–111-31) Noth and [Michael Madsen](/wiki/Michael_Madsen "Michael Madsen") were candidates for the role of Logan. Madsen initially was considered the perfect choice for the role, but, in a final reading, it was felt that Madsen's acting mannerisms were repetitive, and Noth received the role instead.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199925–26-32) Rounding out the police cast, [Dann Florek](/wiki/Dann_Florek "Dann Florek") was cast as Captain [Donald Cragen](/wiki/Donald_Cragen "Donald Cragen").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199925-33)

On the prosecutor's side, [Michael Moriarty](/wiki/Michael_Moriarty "Michael Moriarty") was Dick Wolf's choice to play Executive Assistant District Attorney [Benjamin "Ben" Stone](/wiki/Benjamin_Stone_(Law_%26_Order_character) "Benjamin Stone (Law & Order character)"). The network, however, preferred [James Naughton](/wiki/James_Naughton "James Naughton"), but, in the end, Wolf's choice would prevail, and Moriarty received the role.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199925-33) As his A.D.A., [Richard Brooks](/wiki/Richard_Brooks_(actor) "Richard Brooks (actor)") and [Eriq La Salle](/wiki/Eriq_La_Salle "Eriq La Salle") were being considered for the role of [Paul Robinette](/wiki/Paul_Robinette "Paul Robinette"). The network favored La Salle but, once again, the producers' choice prevailed, and Brooks received the role.[[33]](#cite_note-Courrier_and_Green_26-34) As their boss, [Roy Thinnes](/wiki/Roy_Thinnes "Roy Thinnes") was cast as District Attorney Alfred Wentworth.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199925-33)

### Seasons 1–3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Seasons 1–3")]

Nearly two years passed between the pilot and production of the series. The producers held options on Dzundza, Noth, Moriarty and Brooks. Each was paid holding money for the additional year and brought back. Florek also returned. Thinnes, however, was starring in *[Dark Shadows](/wiki/Dark_Shadows_(1991_TV_series) "Dark Shadows (1991 TV series)")* and declined to return.[[34]](#cite_note-35) In his place, the producers tapped [Steven Hill](/wiki/Steven_Hill "Steven Hill") to portray District Attorney [Adam Schiff](/wiki/Adam_Schiff_(Law_%26_Order) "Adam Schiff (Law & Order)"),[[33]](#cite_note-Courrier_and_Green_26-34) a character loosely based on real life [New York County District Attorney](/wiki/New_York_County_District_Attorney "New York County District Attorney") [Robert Morgenthau](/wiki/Robert_Morgenthau "Robert Morgenthau"). Hill brought prestige and experience to the show, and as such, the producers allowed Hill to give insight on the direction he thought the character should go.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999131–132-36)

### Seasons 4–7

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Seasons 4–7")]

By the end of season 3, NBC executives still felt the show did not have enough female characters. On the orders of then network president [Warren Littlefield](/wiki/Warren_Littlefield "Warren Littlefield"), new female characters had to be added to the cast or the show would face possible cancellation on its relegated Friday nighttime slot. Wolf realized that, since there were only six characters on the show, someone had to be dismissed. He chose to dismiss Florek and Brooks from the regular roster, and later said it was the hardest two phone calls he had ever made. Though producers initially claimed the firings (especially that of Brooks who was reported as not getting along with Moriarty) were for other reasons, Wolf confirmed that the firings were on the orders of Littlefield.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199933–34-37)

To replace Florek, [S. Epatha Merkerson](/wiki/S._Epatha_Merkerson "S. Epatha Merkerson") was cast as new squad leader Lieutenant [Anita Van Buren](/wiki/Anita_Van_Buren "Anita Van Buren"). (Merkerson had previously guest starred as a mother of a gunshot victim in the season 1 episode "Mushrooms".)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999115–117-38) To replace Brooks, [Jill Hennessy](/wiki/Jill_Hennessy "Jill Hennessy") was cast as Assistant District Attorney [Claire Kincaid](/wiki/Claire_Kincaid "Claire Kincaid"). Though no initial explanation was given on the show for the departures of Florek's or Brooks's characters, they would both later return in guest appearances, with Captain Cragen having been reassigned to the Internal Affairs Bureau and A.D.A. Robinette having become a defense attorney. Florek also returned to direct a few episodes, and his character was eventually added to the cast of *[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")*.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999128–131-39)

Meanwhile, Moriarty's behavior both on and off the set became problematic for Wolf. After a public statement in which Moriarty called Attorney General [Janet Reno](/wiki/Janet_Reno "Janet Reno") a "[psychopathic](/wiki/Psychopath "Psychopath") [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi")" for her efforts to censor television violence, Moriarty engaged in a verbal confrontation with Reno at a dinner in Washington, D.C. Wolf asked Moriarty to tone down his comments, and Moriarty responded by quitting the show the next week. The final storyline for Ben Stone involves his resignation over guilt after a woman he compelled to testify against a Russian mobster was murdered by his cohorts. To replace Moriarty, [Sam Waterston](/wiki/Sam_Waterston "Sam Waterston") was Wolf's first choice for the role of Executive Assistant District Attorney [John James "Jack" McCoy Jr.](/wiki/Jack_McCoy "Jack McCoy"); Waterston's character was markedly different from Moriarty's in that Jack McCoy was conceived as more emotionally stable and having more sex appeal.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199935–37-40)

Wolf dismissed Noth when his contract expired at the end of season 5, because he felt that Lennie Briscoe and Mike Logan had become too similar to each other, and the writers were having difficulty in writing their dialogue together. Furthermore, Noth had been disgruntled with the show since the dismissals of Florek and Brooks, and remained embittered against Wolf, who he felt was not a friend to his actors. The final storyline for Detective Logan involved his banishment to work on [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") in a [domestic violence](/wiki/Domestic_violence "Domestic violence") crimes unit as punishment for punching a city council member who had orchestrated the murder of a gay colleague and had managed to get acquitted of the charges. (The made-for-television film *[Exiled: A Law & Order Movie](/wiki/Exiled:_A_Law_%26_Order_Movie "Exiled: A Law & Order Movie")*, in which Noth starred, centers on Logan's attempt to get back into the department's good graces.) Noth was replaced by [Benjamin Bratt](/wiki/Benjamin_Bratt "Benjamin Bratt") as Detective [Reynaldo "Rey" Curtis](/wiki/Rey_Curtis "Rey Curtis"), who was hired in an attempt to find an actor even sexier than Noth to join the cast.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen199934–35-41)

Hennessy chose not to renew her three-year contract at the end of season 6 to pursue other projects, and Claire Kincaid was written off as being killed in a drunk driving crash.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999130-42) She was replaced by [Carey Lowell](/wiki/Carey_Lowell "Carey Lowell") as Assistant District Attorney [Jamie Ross](/wiki/Jamie_Ross_(Law_%26_Order) "Jamie Ross (Law & Order)"). Lowell remained with the show until the end of season 8, when she left to spend more time with her daughter. (Jamie Ross was written off as leaving the D.A.'s office for similar reasons.)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999134–135-43) Lowell (who later returned for a couple of guest appearances) was replaced by [Angie Harmon](/wiki/Angie_Harmon "Angie Harmon") as Assistant District Attorney [Abigail "Abbie" Carmichael](/wiki/Abbie_Carmichael "Abbie Carmichael"), who was conceived as being much louder and outspoken than any of her predecessors. Harmon auditioned with 85 other women, including [Vanessa Williams](/wiki/Vanessa_L._Williams "Vanessa L. Williams"), for the role, and was picked after Wolf heard her Texas accent.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999333–335-44)

### Seasons 8–14

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Seasons 8–14")]

Beginning in season 8 (1997),[[44]](#cite_note-45) [J. K. Simmons](/wiki/J._K._Simmons "J. K. Simmons") had the recurring role of [Dr. Emil Skoda](/wiki/Emil_Skoda_(Law_%26_Order) "Emil Skoda (Law & Order)"), a psychiatrist who worked with the Police Department. He appeared in 41 episodes until 2004. He then reappeared for three episodes in season 20.

Bratt left the series at the end of season 9, stating it was an amicable departure and he expected to eventually return for guest appearances. (He ultimately returned for the season 20 episode "Fed".) Detective Curtis was written off as leaving the force in order to take care of his wife, who was suffering from [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), in her final days.[[45]](#cite_note-Bratt_departure-46) He was replaced by [Jesse L. Martin](/wiki/Jesse_L._Martin "Jesse L. Martin") as Detective [Ed Green](/wiki/Ed_Green_(Law_%26_Order) "Ed Green (Law & Order)"), who was conceived of as more of a loose cannon in the mold of Noth's Logan than Bratt's Curtis was.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourrierGreen1999331–332-47) (Briscoe was described as being a recovering alcoholic, as Cragen had been. Green was described as being a recovering compulsive gambler.) In 2000, Steven Hill announced he was leaving the series after season 10. Hill, who was the last remaining member of the original cast, said his departure was mutual with the producers. He was replaced by [Dianne Wiest](/wiki/Dianne_Wiest "Dianne Wiest") as Interim District Attorney [Nora Lewin](/wiki/Nora_Lewin "Nora Lewin"), and Adam Schiff was written out off-screen as departing to work with Jewish charities and human-rights organizations in Europe.[[47]](#cite_note-Hill_departure-48)

The following year, Harmon left the show after three seasons (with Abbie Carmichael written off as being called on to serve the U.S. Attorney's office) and was replaced by [Elisabeth Röhm](/wiki/Elisabeth_R%C3%B6hm "Elisabeth Röhm") as Assistant District Attorney [Serena Southerlyn](/wiki/Serena_Southerlyn "Serena Southerlyn").[[48]](#cite_note-Harmon_departure-49) The year after that, Wiest left the show after two seasons and was replaced by retiring U.S. Senator [Fred Thompson](/wiki/Fred_Thompson "Fred Thompson") as District Attorney [Arthur Branch](/wiki/Arthur_Branch "Arthur Branch"), whose character was conceived of as being much more right-leaning than his predecessors in the D.A.'s office, and was a direct reaction to the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[49]](#cite_note-Wiest_departure-50) No mention was made on the show of what happened to Nora Lewin, though producers said her character was only supposed to be an interim D.A.

### Seasons 15 & 16

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Seasons 15 & 16")]

After 12 years on *Law & Order*, Orbach announced in March 2004 that he was leaving the show at the end of season 14 for the spin-off *[Law & Order: Trial by Jury](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Trial_by_Jury "Law & Order: Trial by Jury")*. Lennie Briscoe was written off as retiring from the NYPD and later taking a position as an investigator for the D.A.'s office. He was replaced at the 27th Precinct by Detective [Joe Fontana](/wiki/Joe_Fontana_(Law_%26_Order) "Joe Fontana (Law & Order)"), played by [Dennis Farina](/wiki/Dennis_Farina "Dennis Farina").[[50]](#cite_note-Orbach_departure-51) At the time, Orbach would not state the reason for his departure,[[50]](#cite_note-Orbach_departure-51) but it was eventually revealed that he had been battling [prostate cancer](/wiki/Prostate_cancer "Prostate cancer") (for over 10 years) and that his role on *Trial by Jury* was designed to be less taxing on him than his role on the original series was. However, Orbach died from his cancer on December 28, 2004, and was featured in only the first two episodes of *Trial by Jury.* (His character was subsequently written off as having also died off-screen, though this was not revealed on the original series until the season 18 episode "Burn Card".)[[51]](#cite_note-Orbach_death-52)

In Season 15, Röhm left the show mid-season in January 2005. Röhm's final scene on the show, in the episode "Ain't No Love", sparked controversy within the fanbase, as A.D.A. Southerlyn asked Arthur Branch if she was being fired because she was a lesbian, a fact the scripts had never hinted at until then.[[52]](#cite_note-Martin_exit-53) Wolf said Röhm's departure was unexpected. For a few seasons, Southerlyn had often argued opposing points to McCoy and Branch, the latter of whom thought she would be better as a defender rather than a prosecutor. Southerlyn's replacement was [Annie Parisse](/wiki/Annie_Parisse "Annie Parisse") as Assistant District Attorney [Alexandra Borgia](/wiki/Alexandra_Borgia "Alexandra Borgia").

Martin left the show midseason to film *[Rent](/wiki/Rent_(film) "Rent (film)").* Ed Green was temporarily written off as being shot in the line of duty and was replaced during his recovery by Detective Nick Falco, played by [Michael Imperioli](/wiki/Michael_Imperioli "Michael Imperioli"), who had previously guest-starred as a murder suspect in the season 6 episode "Atonement".[[53]](#cite_note-Parisse-Imperioli-54) Parisse left the series at the end of season 16 (with A.D.A. Borgia written off as being murdered), and Farina shortly afterward announced that he was leaving the show to pursue other projects. (Detective Fontana was written off as having retired off-screen.)[[54]](#cite_note-Parisse-Farina-55)

### Seasons 17–20

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Seasons 17–20")]

By this point, NBC executives believed the series was beginning to show its age, as the ratings had been declining since Orbach's departure.[[55]](#cite_note-Govich-de_la_Garza-56) Farina had never been popular with fans when he replaced Orbach, and it was felt that the cast just did not seem to mesh well together anymore.[[52]](#cite_note-Martin_exit-53) In an effort to revitalize the show, Wolf replaced Parisse with [Alana de la Garza](/wiki/Alana_de_la_Garza "Alana de la Garza") as Assistant District Attorney [Consuela "Connie" Rubirosa](/wiki/Connie_Rubirosa "Connie Rubirosa"), while Martin's Green was promoted to senior detective and partnered with Detective Nina Cassady, played by [Milena Govich](/wiki/Milena_Govich "Milena Govich"), who had worked with Wolf on the short-lived series *[Conviction](/wiki/Conviction_(2006_TV_series) "Conviction (2006 TV series)")* and served as the show's first female detective of the main cast.[[55]](#cite_note-Govich-de_la_Garza-56) She also briefly appeared as a bartender in the season 16 episode titled "Flaw".

However, Govich proved to be even more unpopular with fans than her predecessor was and left the show after one season with the explanation being that Detective Cassady's assignment to the precinct had been temporary and had been transferred out. She was replaced by [Jeremy Sisto](/wiki/Jeremy_Sisto "Jeremy Sisto"), who had previously guest starred as a defense attorney in the season 17 episode "The Family Hour", as Detective [Cyrus Lupo](/wiki/Cyrus_Lupo "Cyrus Lupo").[[56]](#cite_note-Cheers_Lupo-57) Around the same time, Thompson announced he would leave the show to seek the [2008 Republican presidential nomination](/wiki/2008_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "2008 Republican Party presidential primaries"). (No explanation was given within the show regarding Arthur Branch's off-screen departure.) Waterston's character was promoted to Interim District Attorney (later made full District Attorney in season 20) and his former position was filled in by Executive Assistant District Attorney [Michael Cutter](/wiki/Michael_Cutter "Michael Cutter"), played by [Linus Roache](/wiki/Linus_Roache "Linus Roache").[[57]](#cite_note-Govich-Thompson-58)[[58]](#cite_note-Ken_Tucker_2008-59)

Martin later announced that he would leave the show for the second and last time near the end of season 18 to pursue other endeavors, and Detective Green was written off as resigning from the force due to burnout. He was replaced by [Anthony Anderson](/wiki/Anthony_Anderson "Anthony Anderson") as Detective [Kevin Bernard](/wiki/Kevin_Bernard "Kevin Bernard").[[52]](#cite_note-Martin_exit-53) In 2010, Merkerson announced that she would leave the show at the end of season 20, with Lieutenant Van Buren given a season-long story arc involving her battling [cervical cancer](/wiki/Cervical_cancer "Cervical cancer").[[59]](#cite_note-Merkerson_exit-60) However, the cancellation of the show rendered this moot.

### Season 21–present (Revival series)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Season 21–present (Revival series)")]

In *Deadline Hollywood*, Nellie Andreeva announced the series was returning.[[60]](#cite_note-eid-61) On November 1, 2021, [Jeffrey Donovan](/wiki/Jeffrey_Donovan "Jeffrey Donovan") was cast as a series regular to portray an NYPD detective, later revealed to be named Frank Cosgrove.[[61]](#cite_note-Jeffrey-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) At that time it was also reported that [Sam Waterston](/wiki/Sam_Waterston "Sam Waterston") and [Anthony Anderson](/wiki/Anthony_Anderson "Anthony Anderson"), who starred in earlier seasons of the series, and additional former cast members were also in talks to return. Waterston previously stated in 2015 that he would be open to returning.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Other previous cast members including [S. Epatha Merkerson](/wiki/S._Epatha_Merkerson "S. Epatha Merkerson"), [Jeremy Sisto](/wiki/Jeremy_Sisto "Jeremy Sisto") and [Alana de la Garza](/wiki/Alana_de_la_Garza "Alana de la Garza") hold starring roles on *[Chicago Med](/wiki/Chicago_Med "Chicago Med")* or *[FBI](/wiki/FBI_(TV_series) "FBI (TV series)")*, with both also being part of the franchise and Wolf Entertainment series.[[60]](#cite_note-eid-61) On November 23, 2021, it was announced that [Hugh Dancy](/wiki/Hugh_Dancy "Hugh Dancy") had been cast as an assistant district attorney and that Anderson had signed a one-year deal to return as Detective [Kevin Bernard](/wiki/Kevin_Bernard "Kevin Bernard").[[65]](#cite_note-Hugh-66) On December 10, 2021, it was revealed that [Camryn Manheim](/wiki/Camryn_Manheim "Camryn Manheim") had been cast as Lieutenant Kate Dixon, the successor to Merkerson's character, Lieutenant [Anita Van Buren](/wiki/Anita_Van_Buren "Anita Van Buren"). Manheim portrayed minor characters in previous seasons of the series.[[66]](#cite_note-Andreeva-67) In December 2021, [Odelya Halevi](/wiki/Odelya_Halevi "Odelya Halevi") was added to the cast as Assistant District Attorney Samantha Maroun.[[67]](#cite_note-Deadline_Hollywood-68) A day later, Waterston was announced to have finalized a one-year deal to return as District Attorney [Jack McCoy](/wiki/Jack_McCoy "Jack McCoy").[[68]](#cite_note-Sam_Waterston_Returns_To_'Law_&_Ord-69)

On May 10, 2022, the series was renewed by NBC for a 22nd season.[[69]](#cite_note-Hailu-70) Later that same month, it was announced that Anderson would leave the series.[[70]](#cite_note-71) On June 7, Waterston signed a new deal to return for the 22nd season, making him the longest-running cast member of the series.[[71]](#cite_note-72) A week later, [Mehcad Brooks](/wiki/Mehcad_Brooks "Mehcad Brooks") joined the cast in the new season, replacing Anderson as Detective Jalen Shaw.[[72]](#cite_note-73) On November 15, 2023, it was announced that Donovan would be not returning for the 23rd season.[[73]](#cite_note-74) A week later, [Reid Scott](/wiki/Reid_Scott_(actor) "Reid Scott (actor)") joined the cast for the 23rd season, replacing Donovan as Detective Vincent Riley.[[74]](#cite_note-75) On February 2, 2024, it was announced that Waterston would depart from the series and [Tony Goldwyn](/wiki/Tony_Goldwyn "Tony Goldwyn") would join the cast as District Attorney Nicholas Baxter.[[75]](#cite_note-76) On May 10, 2024, it was announced [Camryn Manheim](/wiki/Camryn_Manheim "Camryn Manheim") would depart the series after season 23.[[76]](#cite_note-77) On July 22, 2024, [Maura Tierney](/wiki/Maura_Tierney "Maura Tierney") was cast as a new lieutenant for season 24, replacing Manheim as Lieutenant Jessica Brady.[[77]](#cite_note-78) In May 2025, NBC renewed the series for its twenty-fifth season.[[78]](#cite_note-Variety–S25-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) On September 24, 2025, it was revealed that [David Ajala](/wiki/David_Ajala "David Ajala") was set to join the show's main cast as an NYPD detective partway into its 25th season, replacing Brooks.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-law&orderjoinsup-82) Ajala would debut on *Law & Order* as Detective Theo Walker on the November 13, 2025 episode *Guardian*.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Plot")]

> In the criminal justice system, the people are represented by two separate yet equally important groups: the police who investigate crime, and the district attorneys who prosecute the offenders. These are their stories.

—Opening narration, spoken by [Steven Zirnkilton](/wiki/Steven_Zirnkilton "Steven Zirnkilton").[[82]](#cite_note-nbc090923-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-Everybody's_Favorite_Bagman-85)

*Law & Order* episodes are structured into two parts. The initial segment involves police investigations, and the subsequent segment portrays legal and courtroom proceedings. The show focuses on the progression of each case rather than character backstories.

### Police investigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Police investigation")]

In the first half of each episode, the detectives of [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department")'s fictional 27th precinct investigate a violent crime such as a murder, kidnapping, or rape. The police collect evidence and interview witnesses and potential suspects. When the evidence indicates a likely suspect, the police arrest them.

### Legal proceedings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Legal proceedings")]

In the second half of each episode, the prosecutors of the [Manhattan District Attorney](/wiki/New_York_County_District_Attorney "New York County District Attorney")'s office take over. The prosecutors discuss deals and plea bargains, prepare the witnesses and evidence, and represent the people's case in the trial of the accused. The prosecutors take it in turns with the defendant's defense attorney to argue the case before a jury until an outcome is decided.

## "Ripped from the headlines"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: \"Ripped from the headlines\"")]

*Law & Order* episodes are often advertised as being "ripped from the headlines", a slogan that refers to the franchise's practice of conceiving stories that are partially inspired by recent headlines. There might be a few scenes that resemble a well-known headline, while the majority of the episode goes in a different direction, or there could be one character that is based on a famous individual, but the circumstances the person encounters are largely fictional, and the rest of the plot usually diverges significantly from the actual events that may have inspired the episode.[[85]](#cite_note-newsday-86) This "ripped from the headlines" theme is reflected in the opening credits sequence that evolves from newspaper halftones to high-resolution photos. Advertisements of episodes with close real-life case parallels often use the "ripped from the headlines" phrase, although [a textual disclaimer, within the actual episode, emphasizes that the story and characters are fictional](/wiki/All_persons_fictitious_disclaimer "All persons fictitious disclaimer").

Some real-life crime victims have felt used and exploited,[[85]](#cite_note-newsday-86) with one lawyer, Ravi Batra, going so far as to sue the show in 2004 for [libel](/wiki/Libel "Libel") with regard to the season 14 episode "[Floater](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_season_14#ep308 "Law & Order season 14")", which portrayed a lawyer with a similar name and the distinctive features of Batra.[[86]](#cite_note-87) Batra and the show later settled out of court for an unspecified amount.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

## Broadcast history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Broadcast history")]

### Broadcast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Broadcast")]

The show premiered September 13, 1990,[[109]](#cite_note-112) and ended its first run on May 24, 2010. 456 episodes were aired and produced. The show ran for twenty seasons on NBC. In 2002, *Law & Order* surpassed *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")* as the longest-running police drama on American television. At this time, it was NBC's longest running crime drama and tied for longest-running primetime scripted drama with *Gunsmoke*. The first two seasons were broadcast Tuesdays at 10 p.m. From season 3 through 16, the show aired Wednesday at 10 p.m. For season 17, it moved to Fridays at 10 p.m. For seasons 18 and 19, the show shifted back to Wednesdays at 10 p.m. For season 20, the show was broadcast Fridays at 8 p.m., while in the spring it moved to Mondays at 10 p.m., where it broadcast its initial series finale on May 24, 2010. The revival (season 21–present) now airs as part of NBC's 'Law & Order Thursday' lineup broadcasting Thursdays at 8 p.m. since February 24, 2022.

### Syndication and streaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Syndication and streaming")]

Repeats of *Law & Order* were first broadcast weekdays on cable TV network [A&E](/wiki/A%26E_(TV_channel) "A&E (TV channel)") during the 1994–95 season. The A&E broadcasts were credited with drawing a new, much larger audience to the current weekly NBC *Law & Order* episodes. In 2001, A&E did not renew its contract to syndicate *Law & Order* as the price was then four times the original 1995 contract price.[[183]](#cite_note-186) Reruns moved to [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)"), where they remained until 2019.

As of 2023, the series is being broadcast on [Sundance TV](/wiki/Sundance_TV "Sundance TV"), [WE tv](/wiki/WE_tv "WE tv"), [Ion Mystery](/wiki/Ion_Mystery "Ion Mystery"), [BBC America](/wiki/BBC_America "BBC America"), [Bounce TV](/wiki/Bounce_TV "Bounce TV"), [Paramount Network](/wiki/Paramount_Network "Paramount Network") and [Pop](/wiki/Pop_(American_TV_channel) "Pop (American TV channel)").

Since mid-2020, selected seasons of *Law & Order* have been available for streaming on [Peacock](/wiki/Peacock_(streaming_service) "Peacock (streaming service)") along with *[Chicago Fire](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_(TV_series) "Chicago Fire (TV series)")*, *[Chicago P.D.](/wiki/Chicago_P.D._(TV_series) "Chicago P.D. (TV series)")*, *[Chicago Med](/wiki/Chicago_Med "Chicago Med")*, *[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")*, and *[Law & Order: Criminal Intent](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Criminal_Intent "Law & Order: Criminal Intent")*. However, unlike some shows on Peacock such as selected seasons of *SVU*, which are free, access to *Law & Order* requires a paid Peacock subscription.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

As of December 16, 2024, *Law & Order* seasons 1–20 are now available to stream on [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu").

A [free ad-supported streaming television](/wiki/Free_ad-supported_streaming_television "Free ad-supported streaming television") channel featuring *Law & Order* episodes from Seasons 5 through 10 launched in September 2025 on various platforms.[[185]](#cite_note-188)

## Cancellation and revival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Cancellation and revival")]

On May 14, 2010, [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") officially canceled *Law & Order*,[[186]](#cite_note-Variety-189) opting instead to pick up *Law & Order: Los Angeles* as a series and renew *[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")* for a twelfth season.[[2]](#cite_note-canceled-3) Creator Dick Wolf continued to pressure the series' producer [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") to make a deal with [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)"), which held syndication rights to the show, for a twenty-first season if an acceptable license fee could be negotiated. Talks between the two started up after upfronts.[[187]](#cite_note-Deadline_2010-05-17-190) However, TNT said in a statement it was not interested in picking the show up for a new season.[[188]](#cite_note-TNTsaysno-191)

After TNT discussions fell through, cable network [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)") also considered reviving *Law & Order*;[[189]](#cite_note-192) however, attempts to revive it failed, and according to creator Dick Wolf, the series "moved into the history books".[[190]](#cite_note-193)[[191]](#cite_note-showdead-194)[[192]](#cite_note-showdead2-195)

In February 2015, NBC considered bringing the series back for a 10-episode limited series.[[193]](#cite_note-196)[[194]](#cite_note-197)

On September 28, 2021, NBC announced that a 21st season had been ordered.[[6]](#cite_note-TVLineRevive-7) The new season was announced after plans for a new *Law & Order* spin-off, *For the Defense*, had fallen through during the summer.[[195]](#cite_note-198)[[196]](#cite_note-199) On November 1, 2021, it was announced that [Jeffrey Donovan](/wiki/Jeffrey_Donovan "Jeffrey Donovan") was cast as a new series regular, while Sam Waterston and Anthony Anderson would later be announced to return.[[61]](#cite_note-Jeffrey-62) On November 12, 2021, it was announced that the 21st season would premiere on February 24, 2022.[[197]](#cite_note-200)[[7]](#cite_note-Revival_Premiere_Date-8)

On November 23, 2021, it was announced that [Hugh Dancy](/wiki/Hugh_Dancy "Hugh Dancy") would join the cast for the 21st season, and it was announced Anderson would reprise his role as Detective [Kevin Bernard](/wiki/Kevin_Bernard "Kevin Bernard").[[65]](#cite_note-Hugh-66)

On December 10, 2021, it was revealed that [Camryn Manheim](/wiki/Camryn_Manheim "Camryn Manheim") had been cast as Lieutenant Kate Dixon, the successor to Merkerson's character, Lieutenant [Anita Van Buren](/wiki/Anita_Van_Buren "Anita Van Buren"). Manheim portrayed minor characters in previous seasons of the series.[[66]](#cite_note-Andreeva-67) On December 15, 2021, [Odelya Halevi](/wiki/Odelya_Halevi "Odelya Halevi") was announced to be joining the cast as Assistant District Attorney Samantha Maroun.[[67]](#cite_note-Deadline_Hollywood-68) A day later, Waterston was announced to have finalized a one-year deal to return as District Attorney [Jack McCoy](/wiki/Jack_McCoy "Jack McCoy").[[68]](#cite_note-Sam_Waterston_Returns_To_'Law_&_Ord-69) *Law & Order* officially aired its first new episode in almost 12 years on February 24, 2022.

On May 10, 2022, NBC renewed the series for a twenty-second season.[[198]](#cite_note-201) One week after the twenty-first season ended, On May 26, 2022, it was confirmed that Anderson would not be returning for the twenty-second season.[[199]](#cite_note-202) On June 7, 2022, it was announced that Waterston would reprise his role as McCoy for the twenty-second season.[[200]](#cite_note-203) On June 13, 2022, it was reported that [Mehcad Brooks](/wiki/Mehcad_Brooks "Mehcad Brooks") was cast for the twenty-second season.[[201]](#cite_note-204)

On April 10, 2023, the series was renewed for its twenty-third season,[[202]](#cite_note-205)[[203]](#cite_note-206) which premiered on January 18, 2024.[[10]](#cite_note-S23Premiere-11) Two months later, the series was renewed for its twenty-fourth season.[[204]](#cite_note-207) On May 8, 2025, the series was renewed for its twenty-fifth season.[[78]](#cite_note-Variety–S25-79)[[205]](#cite_note-208)

On July 18, 2025, it was announced that Mechad Brooks was leaving after three seasons, a "mutual decision" for both the actor and producers.

## Spin-offs, crossovers, and adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Spin-offs, crossovers, and adaptations")]

Main article: [Law & Order (franchise)](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_(franchise) "Law & Order (franchise)")

The longevity and success of *Law & Order* have spawned six American television series (*[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")*, *[Law & Order: Criminal Intent](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Criminal_Intent "Law & Order: Criminal Intent")*, *[Law & Order: Trial by Jury](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Trial_by_Jury "Law & Order: Trial by Jury")*, *[Law & Order: LA](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_LA "Law & Order: LA")*, *[Law & Order True Crime](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_True_Crime "Law & Order True Crime"), and* *[Law & Order: Organized Crime](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Organized_Crime "Law & Order: Organized Crime"))* as well as a television film (*[Exiled: A Law & Order Movie](/wiki/Exiled:_A_Law_%26_Order_Movie "Exiled: A Law & Order Movie")*). The commercial potential of the *Law & Order* name outweighed initial fears that failed spin-offs (such as *Trial by Jury* and *Los Angeles*) could erode the audience of the original series.[[206]](#cite_note-Green_and_Dawn_10-11-209) To differentiate it from other series in the franchise, *Law & Order* is often referred to as "The Mother Ship" by producers and critics.[[207]](#cite_note-Green_and_Dawn_2-210)

*Law & Order* has had [crossover](/wiki/Crossover_(fiction) "Crossover (fiction)") episodes with other series in its franchise. Additionally, it crossed over with *[New York Undercover](/wiki/New_York_Undercover "New York Undercover")* and *[Conviction](/wiki/Conviction_(2006_TV_series) "Conviction (2006 TV series)")*; while neither series belongs to the *Law & Order* franchise officially, both are part of its [fictional universe](/wiki/Fictional_universe "Fictional universe"), and were also created by Wolf. It also had several crossover episodes with *[Homicide: Life on the Street](/wiki/Homicide:_Life_on_the_Street "Homicide: Life on the Street")*. *Law & Order*'s success has spawned two other external franchises that co-exist in the same universe (*[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_(franchise) "Chicago (franchise)")* and *[FBI](/wiki/FBI_(franchise) "FBI (franchise)")*). *Chicago* and *Law & Order* were connected through *[Chicago Fire](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_(TV_series) "Chicago Fire (TV series)")* and *[Chicago P.D.](/wiki/Chicago_P.D._(TV_series) "Chicago P.D. (TV series)")* with crossovers between *[SVU](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")*. *Chicago P.D.* also had crossovers with *[FBI](/wiki/FBI_(TV_series) "FBI (TV series)")*.

The series has been adapted for British television as *[Law & Order: UK](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_UK "Law & Order: UK")*, with the setting being changed to London. In 2024, a Canadian produced series, *[Law & Order Toronto: Criminal Intent](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_Toronto:_Criminal_Intent "Law & Order Toronto: Criminal Intent")* premiered on [Citytv](/wiki/Citytv "Citytv"); this series is based on the *Criminal Intent* format and set in the city of Toronto.

There have been a few unofficial "rip-offs" of *Law & Order* in other countries. For example, Singapore's version of *Law & Order* was entitled *[Code of Law](/wiki/Code_of_Law "Code of Law")*, starring [Keagan Kang](/wiki/Keagan_Kang "Keagan Kang") and [Joanne Peh](/wiki/Joanne_Peh "Joanne Peh") as Jacob Fernandez and Sabrina Wong (the counterparts to Jack McCoy and Serena Southerlyn respectively from the original *Law & Order*), and aired a total of five seasons of 59 episodes between September 2012 and June 2020.[[208]](#cite_note-211)

## Awards and honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Awards and honors")]

Main article: [List of awards and nominations received by Law & Order](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Law_%26_Order "List of awards and nominations received by Law & Order")

*Law & Order* has been nominated for numerous awards in the television industry over the span of its run.

Among its wins are the 1997 [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series"), [Screen Actors Guild Awards](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Award "Screen Actors Guild Award") for Outstanding Male Actor in a Drama Series for [Sam Waterston](/wiki/Sam_Waterston "Sam Waterston") in 1999 and [Jerry Orbach](/wiki/Jerry_Orbach "Jerry Orbach") in 2005 (awarded after his death), and numerous [Edgar Awards](/wiki/Edgar_Awards "Edgar Awards") for Best Episode in a Television Series Teleplay.

In 2002, *Law & Order* was ranked #24 on [TV Guide's 50 Greatest TV Shows of All Time](/wiki/TV_Guide%27s_50_Greatest_TV_Shows_of_All_Time "TV Guide's 50 Greatest TV Shows of All Time").[[209]](#cite_note-212)[[210]](#cite_note-213) The show also placed #27 on *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*'s "New TV Classics" list.[[211]](#cite_note-214)

In 2013, *[TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")* ranked *Law & Order* #14 on their list of the 60 Greatest Shows of All Time.[[212]](#cite_note-215)

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Law_%26_Order&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Home media")]

A box set titled *Law & Order Producer's Collection* was released on [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS") in 2000.[[213]](#cite_note-216) The 3-tape set included six episodes of the series.

[Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Pictures_Home_Entertainment "Universal Pictures Home Entertainment") has separately released all twenty seasons on DVD in Region 1, along with the complete series in a box set. Law & Order: The Complete Series boxed set features all 20 seasons from the original 1990-2010 run. Each season is individually packaged (in tray-stack style), with all new cover-art (including new cover art for the seasons that have been released). The set also includes a 50-page full-color book titled "The Episode Guide". Along with episode names and synopsis, there is trivia, facts about the making of the show, liner notes, and over 80 full-color photos. In Region 2, Universal Playback has released the first seven seasons on DVD in the UK. In Region 4, [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") has released all 20 seasons on DVD in Australia and New Zealand.
